is the set of notes the player should or can achieve while playing. All instruments have a designated range, and all pitched instruments have a playing range. From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Pitch range of musical instruments

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Sax_range.svg/250px-Sax_range.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Sax_range.svg)

Written range of a [saxophone](/wiki/Saxophone "Saxophone").

In [music](/wiki/Music "Music"), the **range**, or **chromatic range**, of a [musical instrument](/wiki/Musical_instrument "Musical instrument") is the distance from the lowest to the highest [pitch](/wiki/Pitch_(music) "Pitch (music)") it can play. For a singing [voice](/wiki/Register_(music) "Register (music)"), the equivalent is [vocal range](/wiki/Vocal_range "Vocal range"). The range of a [musical part](/wiki/Musical_part "Musical part") is the distance between its lowest and highest [note](/wiki/Note_(music) "Note (music)").

## Compass

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Range_(music)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Compass")]

Among [British English](/wiki/British_English "British English") speakers,[[1]](#cite_note-1) and perhaps others,[[2]](#cite_note-2) **compass** means the same thing as chromatic range—the interval between the lowest and highest note attainable by a voice or musical instrument.

## Other ranges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Range_(music)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Other ranges")]

The terms **sounding range**, **written range**, **designated range**, **duration range** and **[dynamic range](/wiki/Dynamic_range "Dynamic range")** have specific meanings.

The **sounding range**[[3]](#cite_note-M29-3) refers to the pitches produced by an instrument, while the **written range**[[3]](#cite_note-M29-3) refers to the compass (span) of notes written in the sheet music, where the part is sometimes [transposed](/wiki/Transposition_(music) "Transposition (music)") for convenience. A [piccolo](/wiki/Piccolo "Piccolo"), for example, typically has a sounding range one [octave](/wiki/Octave "Octave") higher than its written range.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The **designated range** is the set of notes the player should or can achieve while playing. All instruments have a designated range, and all pitched instruments have a playing range. Timbre, dynamics, and duration ranges are interrelated and one may achieve registral range at the expense of timbre. The designated range is thus the range in which a player is expected to have comfortable control of all aspects.

The **[duration](/wiki/Duration_(music) "Duration (music)") range** is the difference between the shortest and longest rhythm used. **Dynamic range** is the difference between the quietest and loudest volume of an instrument, part or piece of music.

## Range limits

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Range_(music)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Range limits")]

Although [woodwind instruments](/wiki/Woodwind_instrument "Woodwind instrument") and [string instruments](/wiki/String_instrument "String instrument") have no theoretical upper limit to their range (subject to practical limits), they generally cannot go below their designated range. [Brass instruments](/wiki/Brass_instrument "Brass instrument"), on the other hand, can play beyond their designated ranges. Notes lower than the brass instrument's designated range are called [pedal tones](/wiki/Pedal_tone "Pedal tone"). The playing range of a brass instrument depends on both the technical limitations of the instrument and the skill of the player.

Classical arrangements rarely make woodwind or brass instruments play beyond their designed range. String musicians play the bottom of their ranges very frequently, but the top of a string instrument's range is rather fuzzy, and it is unusual for a string player to exceed the designated range. It is quite rare for wind musicians to play the extremes of their instruments. The most common exception is that in many 20th century works, pedal tones are called for in bass trombones.

This chart uses standard numberings for octaves where [middle C](/wiki/Middle_C "Middle C") corresponds to C4. In the [MIDI](/wiki/MIDI "MIDI") language, middle C is referred to as MIDI note number 60.

The lowest note that a [pipe organ](/wiki/Pipe_organ "Pipe organ") can sound (with a [true pipe](/wiki/Organ_pipe "Organ pipe")) is C−1 (or CCCC), which is 8 Hz, [below the range of human hearing](/wiki/Infrasound "Infrasound") and not visible on this chart. However, if acoustic combination (a note and its fifth) counts, the lowest note is C−2 (or CCCCC), which is 4 Hz.

In terms of recording and reproduction, many speakers have a low limit of around 40–60 Hz.

## Typical ranges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Range_(music)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Typical ranges")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | **The [accessibility](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Accessibility "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Accessibility") of this article is in question**. The specific issue is: **Text is way too small and distorted to be legible at default size**. Relevant discussion may be found on the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Range_(music) "Talk:Range (music)"). |

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/timeline/6x26gmv1uo1npmpom42agy7bkysao79.png)

**\***This chart only displays down to C0, though some pipe organs, such as the [Boardwalk Hall Auditorium Organ](/wiki/Boardwalk_Hall_Auditorium_Organ#Diaphone-Dulzian "Boardwalk Hall Auditorium Organ"), extend down to C−1 (one octave below C0). Also, the fundamental frequency of the [subcontrabass tuba](/wiki/Subcontrabass_tuba "Subcontrabass tuba") is B♭−1.
